stories.headline: Republican Gubernatorial Candidates Kirkmeyer and Bottoms Clash in First Televised Primary Debate
stories.summary:
State Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer of Brighton and State Rep. Scott Bottoms of Colorado Springs faced off Thursday in their first televised debate ahead of the June 30 Republican gubernatorial primary. The debate, co-sponsored by The Denver Post, Denver7 and Colorado Public Radio, covered state budget priorities, allegiance to President Donald Trump, and unsubstantiated claims by Bottoms about pedophilia rings operating in Colorado.

Kirkmeyer leaned heavily on her experience as a member of the powerful Joint Budget Committee, noting she has sponsored 68 bills during the 2026 legislative session and regularly works across the aisle. 'I'm the better candidate because I have the most experience and I'm qualified and I know how to get the job done,' she said.

Bottoms, lead pastor at the Church at Briargate in Colorado Springs, repeatedly raised concerns about pedophilia and child trafficking but acknowledged he could not substantiate the claims. In four years in the legislature, Bottoms has been the primary sponsor on one bill that became law — a 2023 measure creating a new license plate.

The winner of the Republican primary will face either Attorney General Phil Weiser or U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet in the general election. A third Republican candidate, Victor Marx, did not participate in Thursday's debate. Colorado has trended increasingly Democratic in recent cycles; Gov. Jared Polis defeated his Republican opponent by more than 19 percentage points in 2022.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis Signs Revised Colorado AI Law, Scaling Back Impact Assessment Requirements
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed Senate Bill 26-189 on Thursday, revising Colorado's landmark artificial intelligence algorithmic discrimination law that had drawn intense scrutiny from the tech industry and business community since its original passage. The new law removes mandatory impact assessment requirements and adds a right-to-cure provision for companies found to be in violation.

The original law, passed in 2024, was considered one of the most sweeping state-level AI regulations in the country. Critics argued its compliance requirements were overly burdensome for businesses and could drive technology companies away from Colorado. Supporters of the revision said the changes strike a better balance between consumer protections and business practicality.

The legislature also advanced a separate trio of AI bills this session targeting health insurance, psychotherapy services and chatbot disclosures. Colorado has positioned itself as an early mover on AI regulation, and the revisions signal an ongoing effort to refine that framework as the technology evolves.

stories.headline: Feds Release $47 Million for Colorado Water Projects After Months-Long Freeze
stories.summary:
The Trump administration announced this week it will release $47 million in federal funding for four Colorado water projects aimed at addressing drought in the Colorado River Basin, ending a months-long freeze that left project applicants in limbo. The Biden administration had pledged up to $152 million in January 2025 for Colorado projects under the Inflation Reduction Act, but the Trump administration froze the grants on its first day in office pending policy review.

The largest portion — $25.6 million — will go to the Southwestern Water Conservation District to fund 17 projects in southwestern Colorado focused on ecosystem restoration, irrigation infrastructure repair and drought resilience. An additional $4.6 million was released for wetland restoration and floodplain improvements in western Colorado. Project managers caution that contracts with the Bureau of Reclamation must still be finalized before funds reach the state, a process expected to take several more months.

The funding release comes amid a historically dry and warm winter that has prompted early water shortages for some Colorado users and widespread concern about water availability for homes, businesses and ecosystems this summer. The Colorado River Basin supplies water to approximately 40 million people and has faced prolonged drought and warming temperatures for decades. The remaining funding from the original $152 million pledge has not yet been released.

stories.headline: Denver Pays $225,000 Settlement After Officers Wrongfully Detained Couple at Gunpoint, Accidentally Fired Weapon
stories.summary:
The City and County of Denver has agreed to pay $225,000 to settle a civil lawsuit filed by Jessica Sjostrom and Rex Hickman, who were mistakenly detained at gunpoint by Denver Police officers during a 2024 felony traffic stop that ended with an officer accidentally discharging his weapon. The settlement was announced at Monday's Denver City Council meeting and brings the city's total taxpayer-funded legal settlements for 2026 to $784,500.

The incident occurred May 26, 2024, near Brighton Boulevard and 29th Street in Denver's RiNo neighborhood. Officers were searching for a man threatening someone with a gun while driving a red Saturn with Colorado plates. Instead, Officer Kirk Malone stopped Sjostrom and Hickman, who were in a maroon Mercury with Kansas plates. Malone fired his weapon during the stop, later telling investigators he accidentally discharged the gun after getting a 'bad grip' while exiting his patrol vehicle.

Police records show officers realized during the stop they had detained the wrong people, yet Sjostrom and Hickman said they were still handcuffed and held. 'They knew it wasn't us, and they continued to arrest us,' Sjostrom previously told 9NEWS. The lawsuit alleged excessive force and failure to intervene by other officers present.

The Denver District Attorney's Office declined to file criminal charges against Malone. Defense attorneys maintained officers had reasonable suspicion to detain the plaintiffs while investigating a 911 report of an attempted murder suspect whose vehicle resembled the one they stopped.

stories.headline: Huntington Bank Opens Two Denver-Area Branches, Names Local Senior Market President
stories.summary:
Huntington Bank has opened two new Colorado branches in the Denver area as part of a broader regional expansion, the bank announced this week. The Ohio-based national bank also named Keith Michalkewicz as senior market president to lead its Denver operations.

The openings represent Huntington's continued push into Western markets. The bank has been expanding its footprint across the country following its 2021 acquisition of TCF Financial Corporation, which added branches in several Midwestern and Mountain states. Specific branch locations were not disclosed in initial reports.

The Denver metro banking market remains competitive, with regional and national institutions vying for commercial and retail customers in one of the country's faster-growing metropolitan areas.

stories.headline: 20th Annual Colfax Marathon Brings 28,000 Runners to Denver Sunday, Triggering Extensive Road Closures
stories.summary:
The 20th Colfax Marathon and its associated races are set to take place Sunday with more than 28,000 participants expected, making it Denver's largest running weekend of the year. The event includes a full 26.2-mile marathon, half-marathon, 10-miler and marathon relay; the half-marathon and 10-miler have already sold out.

Extensive road closures will be in effect from 5 a.m. until 1 p.m. Sunday, with some streets reopening earlier on a rolling basis. East Colfax Avenue will be closed between Colorado Boulevard and Garrison Street in Lakewood, and downtown closures will affect 17th Street and portions of Larimer Street. Drivers are advised to plan alternate routes.

'A 20-year milestone deserves a big celebration,' said Andrea Dowdy, chief executive of the Denver Colfax Marathon. 'There is so much excitement in the city right now, and we are grateful to our runners and our community for growing this tradition.' RTD has separately warned of bus and light rail detours and delays related to the event.

stories.headline: Eagle County Wildfire Near Cordillera Breaks Out With Multiple Ignition Points, Quickly Contained
stories.summary:
A wildfire with multiple separate start points broke out Wednesday morning near Squaw Creek Road and Highway 6 in Eagle County, prompting road closures and a public warning from the Eagle County Sheriff's Office. The fire reached approximately 10 acres before 60 responding firefighters brought it under control by midday, according to officials.

The blaze burned on private land near a sanitation plant in the area. Power was temporarily disrupted for nearby residents but was being restored, and Highway 6 was reopened. Residents of the Cordillera neighborhood were not ordered to evacuate but were told they could not re-enter if they chose to leave; Squaw Creek Road remained closed southbound as of the latest update.

The cause of the fire remained under investigation. The incident comes as Eagle County is experiencing Stage 4 drought — the most severe level — with similarly dire drought conditions in surrounding counties, raising concerns about wildfire risk across Colorado's mountain communities heading into summer.

stories.headline: Popular Berkeley Neighborhood Italian Restaurant Parisi Closed Indefinitely After Electrical Fire
stories.summary:
Parisi and Parisi Sotto, a well-regarded Italian restaurant at 4401 Tennyson St. in Denver's Berkeley neighborhood, are closed indefinitely following an electrical fire earlier this week. The blaze sparked in the crawlspace between the ceiling and rooftop, leaving the building without power, gas or water and damaging the roof during firefighting efforts.

Restaurant officials posted photos and video to social media showing debris, ash, water and fallen plaster strewn across the dining room, with water-damaged paint peeling from the walls. 'I'm gutted right now,' the restaurant's statement read. 'This place is like my first born. … We will be back, but just not sure yet how long it will take.'

No injuries were reported. A timeline for repairs and reopening had not been announced as of Thursday morning.

stories.headline: Havana Street in North Denver to Close for Railroad Track Replacement Starting Monday
stories.summary:
Denver's Department of Transportation and Infrastructure will close portions of Havana Street over the next several weeks for Union Pacific Railroad track maintenance work. The first phase begins Monday and runs through Friday, closing Havana Street between 51st and 56th Avenues for replacement of railroad tracks, installation of a new crossing surface and repaving of crossing approaches.

A second phase is scheduled from June 1 through June 5, closing Havana from 49th to 51st Avenues. Both 51st and 56th Avenues will remain open for east-west travel during the first closure. Motorists will be detoured to Central Park Boulevard for both phases. Work hours are 7 a.m. Monday through noon Friday each week.

stories.headline: Venue Cancels Denver 'Unvaxxed Singles' Mixer Over Safety Concerns; Organizers Seek New Location
stories.summary:
Recess Beer Garden at 2715 17th St. in Denver has canceled a singles mixer organized by Unjected, a dating app marketed to unvaccinated individuals, citing escalating safety concerns and lack of compliance from event organizers. The event had been scheduled for May 29 as part of a four-city tour by the platform, which bills itself as 'the world's first unvaccinated platform.'

The beer garden said it learned of the event through social media and had not agreed to host it. In a TikTok statement, the business cited 'increasingly hostile rhetoric,' online threats and harassment of staff as reasons for the cancellation. 'The safety and well-being of our staff and guests will always come first,' the business wrote, noting it has a strict non-discrimination policy.

Unjected announced on Instagram it is still planning to hold the Denver event on May 29 and is seeking an alternative venue, asking followers to suggest spaces 'owned by someone who actually believes in freedom of choice.' A user identified as Scott Durland, owner of The Grizzly Rose, publicly volunteered his venue in response.

stories.headline: Colorado Defense Suppliers Face Extinction-Level Compliance Costs Under New Pentagon Cybersecurity Rule
stories.summary:
A federal cybersecurity certification requirement taking full effect in November 2026 threatens to push hundreds of Colorado small defense subcontractors out of the market, according to an analysis published by the Colorado Sun. The Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification, or CMMC, now required in Pentagon solicitations, will mandate independent third-party assessment for contractors handling sensitive information at an estimated all-in cost of $150,000 to $400,000 per three-year cycle.

Colorado's aerospace and defense sector employs 55,000 people and generates $23 billion annually in federal contracts. The supply chain beneath major prime contractors such as Lockheed Martin in Littleton, BAE Systems in Boulder and Raytheon in Aurora is dominated by small machine shops, electronics suppliers, testing labs and software subcontractors — firms for which compliance costs could equal or exceed gross margins on defense work.

Industry analysts project that 33,000 to 44,000 firms nationally — 15 to 20 percent of the defense industrial base — could exit the market between 2025 and 2027, with Tier 3 and Tier 4 subcontractors bearing the heaviest losses. Fewer than 1,000 organizations across the entire defense base have achieved the required certification to date, roughly one percent of those expected to need it.

The analysis argues Colorado has tools to intervene — including state-funded compliance assistance or cost-sharing programs — but has not yet acted. Without intervention, the state risks losing a significant portion of a supply chain that underpins its largest economic sector.

stories.headline: Downed Power Lines Block University Boulevard in Greenwood Village, Disrupting Traffic
stories.summary:
Power lines collapsed across South University Boulevard in Greenwood Village on Wednesday afternoon, blocking traffic and prompting a response from South Metro Fire Rescue and Greenwood Village Police. The electrical emergency was first reported at approximately 2:14 p.m., with lines down between East Belleview Avenue and East Orchard Road.

Authorities advised drivers to avoid the area and use alternate routes. The cause of the downed lines and a timeline for restoration were not immediately available. No injuries were reported in connection with the incident.

weather.notable: About 14 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I shouldn't be telling you this. But then, I shouldn't be telling you anything. I'm not that interesting.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Colfax Marathon Weekend
weekendEvents.description: The 20th annual Denver Colfax Marathon returns with more than 28,000 expected participants across a full marathon, half marathon, urban 10-miler, 5K, and marathon relay. The weekend opens with a runner expo at Empower Field at Mile High on Friday and Saturday, with all races except the 5K taking place Sunday.
weekendEvents.time: Expo Friday and Saturday; races begin 6 a.m. Sunday
weekendEvents.location: Empower Field at Mile High (Expo); City Park, Denver (race start)

weekendEvents.title: Bodies: The Science Within — Opening Weekend
weekendEvents.description: This national touring exhibition makes its U.S. debut in Denver, taking visitors on an immersive journey through real human specimens carefully preserved to reveal the body's intricate systems. The limited engagement opens this weekend at Denver Pavilions.

weekendEvents.title: Northglenn Food Truck Carnival
weekendEvents.description: The 9th annual Food Truck Carnival features more than 20 food trucks, over 15 carnival rides, live music, and a kids market at E.B. Rains Park. The family-friendly event runs all three days of the weekend with free admission; carnival ride wristbands are $35.
weekendEvents.time: Friday 4-9 p.m.; Saturday noon-9 p.m.; Sunday noon-5 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: E.B. Rains Park, 11701 Community Center Drive, Northglenn

weekendEvents.title: Sun Valley Viaduct Night Market
weekendEvents.description: This community market transforms the space under the Sun Valley Viaduct into a lively outdoor event with food vendors, local craft vendors, live music, a beer garden, and a roller rink inside a rainbow dome. The event is free and family-friendly.

weekendEvents.title: Erie Town Fair
weekendEvents.description: The 29th annual Erie Town Fair brings more than 200 vendors to Historic Old Town Erie for arts, crafts, food, kids activities, and a beer garden. The one-day event is a long-running community tradition in the northern metro.

weekendEvents.title: Arvada Center Spring Pottery Sale
weekendEvents.description: The Arvada Center's annual Spring Pottery Sale features handmade pottery, sculptures, ceramic jewelry, and decorative pieces created by ceramists, faculty, and advanced students. Admission is free and the sale runs through the weekend.

weekendEvents.title: Pam Grier Celebration Event at Alamo Drafthouse Littleton
weekendEvents.description: Alamo Drafthouse's Littleton location officially relaunches as The Pam Grier Cinema with a live celebration honoring the Denver native and film icon. The event includes a 35mm double feature of 'Foxy Brown' and 'Jackie Brown.'

weekendEvents.title: Echoes of Home: A Festival of Palestinian Film and Culture
weekendEvents.description: This two-day community festival features film screenings, moderated conversations, cultural workshops, poetry, traditional Dabke dance, a pop-up cultural museum, and a marketplace with Palestinian art, food, and handmade goods. Friday evening opens with a cultural gathering and screening of 'The Voice of Hind Rajab' at 7 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Rocky Mountain Theatre for Kids, 5311 Western Avenue, Suite 135, Boulder

weekendEvents.title: Sunset Sessions at The Outpost on Platte
weekendEvents.description: Denver-based clothing brand 'Iim good at this' hosts a free outdoor pop-up event featuring live DJs, local vendors, and beer deals from Denver Beer Co. The event is part of a twelve-week summer series.
weekendEvents.location: The Outpost on Platte, 1635 Platte Street, Denver

topStory.headline: Trump and Xi Conclude Two-Day Beijing Summit With Trade Pledges, Iran Assurances and Unresolved Taiwan Tensions
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump wrapped up a two-day summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing on Friday, declaring that the two leaders had settled "a lot of different problems" and reached what he described as "fantastic trade deals for both countries." The meetings took place at Zhongnanhai, the Chinese leadership compound, and produced agreements covering agricultural trade, with U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer telling Bloomberg TV that a deal involving double-digit billions of dollars in agriculture sales to China was expected as a result of the summit.

Iran emerged as a significant topic at the summit. Trump said he and Xi "feel very similar" on Iran, with both leaders agreeing they do not want Tehran to obtain a nuclear weapon and both expressing a desire to see the Strait of Hormuz — a critical oil shipping chokepoint effectively closed since the U.S.-Iran conflict began in late February — reopened. Trump told Fox News that Xi personally assured him China would not provide military equipment to Iran, calling it "a big statement," though Trump acknowledged Xi also noted China's dependence on Iranian oil imports. The White House formally confirmed that the two leaders had agreed on the importance of keeping the Hormuz strait open.

Taiwan remained a point of divergence. A Chinese readout of the summit indicated Xi warned Trump that mishandling Taiwan could lead to conflict and jeopardize bilateral ties, while a U.S. readout made no mention of Taiwan at all. Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One that he does not believe conflict with China over Taiwan is imminent, saying "We'll be fine," but he declined to say whether a proposed $14 billion U.S. arms package to Taiwan would proceed, stating he would need to consult with Taiwanese President Lai Ching-te before making a final decision. Secretary of State Marco Rubio separately told NBC News that U.S. policy on Taiwan had "not changed" and remained consistent across multiple presidential administrations, a statement Taiwan's Foreign Ministry publicly welcomed.

The summit's aftermath is drawing attention from other world capitals. The Kremlin said President Vladimir Putin's upcoming visit to China — expected soon — would offer an opportunity to exchange views on Trump's Beijing meetings, with spokesman Dmitry Peskov noting the significance of dialogue between the world's top two economies. Trump also spoke by phone with Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi while en route home to Alaska, a call Japan's government characterized as underscoring the importance of the U.S.-Japan alliance amid shifting U.S.-China dynamics. The year-long trade truce between Washington and Beijing is set to expire in October, and Greer indicated both sides have shown willingness to extend it if cooperation continues to prove mutually beneficial.
